evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Kestrel VMC-850, record the spindle chiller complaint as coolant dripping from the rear column only during circulation, residue collecting on the lower cable tray, or coolant loss that appears after a sustained 8000 to 12000 rpm spindle hold. Note the as-received coolant level, spindle speed window, and the first visible drip path before any guard is removed. |
| T2 | Isolate the machine as required and expose spindle-chiller supply hose HC-S1, return hose HC-R1, worm-drive clamps CL-S1 and CL-R1, brass tube necks, chiller manifold block, P-clip PC-RC1 on the rear column, and the lower drip tray. Keep the upper outlet hose and lower return hose visible together so upstream and downstream wetting can be separated instead of being read as one large stain. |
| T3 | Trace the wet boundary from the lowest drip point back toward the upper hose bead, the clamp screw housing, the molded hose bend, or the manifold face, and document whether the stain runs front to rear along the cable tray or drops straight down from the hose neck. Preserve hose twist and clip position because a side-loaded branch often leaks only when the machine is in its final supported geometry. |
| T4 | Pressure-test the branch at 1.5 bar with the spindle stopped and compare the result with the dry reference hose on the opposite side of the chiller block. If branch HC-R1 stays dry under static pressure but wets the lower tray once the chiller pump runs, keep the evidence on vibration load, clamp position, and hose insertion depth rather than replacing the manifold on assumption. |
| T5 | Measure clamp position relative to the tube bead and check hose insertion depth at both the upper outlet neck and the lower return neck. A clamp that sits more than a few millimeters behind the bead or a hose that is pulled rearward by the P-clip can look seated while still opening a leak path once pump pulsation and spindle vibration act on the branch together. |
| T6 | Run the chiller pump and hold the spindle at the complaint speed with the rear guard still open, then watch the upper clamp, the molded bend, and the P-clip bracket through the full vibration event. If wetting starts only when the hose is tugged toward the rear column clip, isolate the fault boundary to the upper return-hose clamp and clip-to-bracket side load rather than to the manifold casting or the lower drain path. |
| T7 | Reposition the hose fully against the tube bead, rotate the clamp so the screw housing does not contact the column bracket, and restore the branch through the original clip path with a relaxed bend radius. Confirm that the hose is seated against the bead, the clamp band is centered, and the P-clip is holding the branch without twisting the upper neck sideways. |
| T8 | Release the machine only after a repeated pressure hold and a repeated high-speed circulation run leave the upper hose neck, lower return path, and drip tray dry. The accepted closeout is a named leak boundary at return-hose clamp CL-R1 or support path PC-RC1, with no renewed coolant trace from upper outlet to lower tray under the same speed window that created the complaint. |

task.surface_form: On Kestrel VMC-850, record the spindle chiller complaint as coolant dripping from the rear column only during circulation, residue collecting on the lower cable tray, or coolant loss that appears after a sustained 8000 to 12000 rpm spindle hold. Note the as-received coolant level, spindle speed window, and the first visible drip path before any guard is removed.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Arcline VTC-630, record the complaint as tool pull-out during face milling, weak unclamp feel, or drawbar alarm on a CAT40 spindle, and note the toolholder style, cutting load, and whether the issue followed recent drawbar service. The intake note must already tie the problem to the drawbar clamp stack and not to a generic spindle fault. |
| T2 | Warm the spindle to the service temperature if required, then expose drawbar booster cylinder CY-DB1, Belleville stack housing BS-DB1, unclamp valve SV-UC1, pressure transducer port PT-UC1, and the access path to the spindle pull-force test point. Keep the upper actuator side and lower retention side visible in the same inspection window so stroke and clamp force can be judged against the same hardware state. |
| T3 | Install the calibrated clamp-force gauge in a verified CAT40 holder, run the clamp cycle, and record the measured pull force rather than relying on sound or feel. Compare the cold and warmed values if the complaint changes with temperature, because a weak Belleville stack and a short hydraulic stroke do not deteriorate in the same way. |
| T4 | Measure unclamp pressure at booster cylinder CY-DB1 through port PT-UC1 and compare it with the machine target while watching drawbar movement. If the supply pressure is healthy but clamp force remains low, the next split should stay inside the mechanical stack, whereas low pressure with normal stack height pushes the boundary toward the hydraulic side of the circuit. |
| T5 | Inspect the Belleville stack height, washer orientation, release-piston clearance, and actuator linkage from upper cylinder to lower drawbar seat. Pay attention to whether one washer pair is inverted or whether the stack height is short at the lower end, because a single reversed pair can reduce clamp force without leaving obvious damage on the piston or pressure circuit. |
| T6 | Compare measured stack height and washer sequence with the service layout, then isolate the fault boundary only after pressure, stroke, and stack geometry are read together. If pressure reaches the target and release stroke is normal while pull force stays low, keep the fault on the Belleville stack orientation or drawbar hardware instead of blaming the valve or pressure supply. |
| T7 | Rebuild the stack in the correct order, restore the actuator linkage, and repeat the pull-force test with the same toolholder and the same spindle temperature window. Confirm that the stack sits against the lower seat, the linkage travels freely, and the pull-force gauge now follows the corrected washer orientation rather than random warm-up variation. |
| T8 | Run three clamp and unclamp cycles while watching pressure recovery, release stroke, and holder retention, then inspect the actuator and cylinder for fresh oil trace or misalignment. The machine is not released on one good force reading if the drawbar still hangs up on the second or third cycle. |
| T9 | Close the work only after the report names the exact failed boundary, such as an inverted lower Belleville pair in BS-DB1, reduced stack height, hydraulic short-stroke at CY-DB1, or actuator-linkage misalignment, and ties it to measured force and pressure results. Accept the repair when clamp force, release stroke, and repeated tool retention all remain stable on the corrected drawbar stack. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: On Arcline VTC-630, record the complaint as tool pull-out during face milling, weak unclamp feel, or drawbar alarm on a CAT40 spindle, and note the toolholder style, cutting load, and whether the issue followed recent drawbar service. The intake note must already tie the problem to the drawbar clamp stack and not to a generic spindle fault.

concept_mentions.label: CAT40 spindle
concept_mentions.description: spindle type with CAT40 toolholder interface

concept_mentions.label: drawbar clamp stack
concept_mentions.description: assembly including Belleville stack, actuator, and drawbar

concept_mentions.label: tool pull-out
concept_mentions.description: complaint: tool pulling out during milling

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Novaris VMC-810, record the warm-up complaint as Y-axis vibration or chatter during reversal between 200 mm and 240 mm from home after approximately fifteen minutes of cycling, and note the feed rate, payload, and coolant state. This file is tied to the Y-axis ball-screw and rear support hardware, not to the spindle or the machine generally. |
| T2 | Run the same Y-axis motion window that produced the complaint and observe rear fixed bearing block BR-YR1, ball-screw shaft, nut housing, rear linear-guide trucks, way-cover segment, and lube manifold while the machine warms. Keep rear support and moving nut housing visible together so the vibration can be tied to one motion boundary instead of to background machine noise. |
| T3 | Measure vibration at rear bearing block BR-YR1 and compare it with the value at mid-travel or on X-axis reference condition, then note screw-surface temperature at sensor TS-YS when the complaint appears. A vibration peak that grows only after the rear support warms points to preload or support loss, while a constant vibration at all temperatures points elsewhere. |
| T4 | Inspect the rear locknut, bearing cap, way-cover leaf, guide-truck lubrication marks, and the screw-to-cover clearance for evidence of fretting, paint shift, dry track, or cover rub. Compare upper and lower way-cover contact marks so a loose support bearing is not confused with a cover leaf that is only grazing the screw near reversal. |
| T5 | Measure axial float or backlash at the Y-axis screw while the machine remains warm, then compare that value with the cold or accepted reference. If axial float at the rear support exceeds the expected band and vibration drops when the support is preloaded by hand, isolate the fault boundary to the rear fixed-bearing locknut or bearing-preload stack rather than to the nut housing or servo tuning. |
| T6 | Restore rear bearing preload, lock the nut, and verify that the support block sits squarely against the saddle casting with the lube line and way-cover segment returned to their original paths. Confirm that the cover leaf no longer touches the screw and that the rear guide trucks remain lubricated rather than dry at the same travel zone. |
| T7 | Repeat the warm-up cycle through the same feed range and reversal points, then compare hot vibration, screw temperature, and backlash with the pre-repair values. The corrected condition must hold through the actual motion window that created the complaint, not just during a short cold jog near home. |
| T8 | Inspect the rear bearing block, way-cover leaf, lube manifold fittings, and guide-truck area again after the repeated motion cycle because settling after travel often reveals a loose fastener or displaced cover segment. This second inspection confirms that the restored preload did not simply shift the symptom to an adjacent support part. |
| T9 | Close the work only after the record names the exact boundary, such as rear fixed-bearing preload at BR-YR1, locknut movement, cover interference, guide-truck lubrication loss, or no defect found at the checked support, and ties it to measured vibration and axial float. Accept the repair when Y-axis reversal remains smooth through the original warm motion window and the rear support hardware holds its corrected position. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: On Novaris VMC-810, record the warm-up complaint as Y-axis vibration or chatter during reversal between 200 mm and 240 mm from home after approximately fifteen minutes of cycling, and note the feed rate, payload, and coolant state. This file is tied to the Y-axis ball-screw and rear support hardware, not to the spindle or the machine generally.

Keep rear support and moving nut housing visible together so the vibration can be tied to one motion boundary instead of to background machine noise.

task.surface_form: Measure vibration at rear bearing block BR-YR1 and compare it with the value at mid-travel or on X-axis reference condition, then note screw-surface temperature at sensor TS-YS when the complaint appears. task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Inspect the rear locknut, bearing cap, way-cover leaf, guide-truck lubrication marks, and the screw-to-cover clearance for evidence of fretting, paint shift, dry track, or cover rub. Compare upper and lower way-cover contact marks so a loose support bearing is not confused with a cover leaf that is only grazing the screw near reversal.

step_records.step_summary: Inspect the rear locknut, bearing cap, way-cover leaf, guide-truck lubri
step_records.surface_form: Inspect the rear locknut, bearing cap, way-cover leaf, guide-truck lubrication marks, and the screw-to-cover clearance for evidence of fretting, paint shift, dry track, or cover rub. Compare upper and lower way-cover contact marks so a loose support bearing is not confused with a cover leaf that is only grazing the screw near reversal.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Measure axial float or backlash at the Y-axis screw while the machine remains warm, then compare that value with the cold or accepted reference. If axial float at the rear support exceeds the expected band and vibration drops when the support is preloaded by hand, isolate the fault boundary to the rear fixed-bearing locknut or bearing-preload stack rather than to the nut housing or servo tuning.

step_records.surface_form: Measure axial float or backlash at the Y-axis screw while the machine remains warm, then compare that value with the cold or accepted reference. If axial float at the rear support exceeds the expected band and vibration drops when the support is preloaded by hand, isolate the fault boundary to the rear fixed-bearing locknut or bearing-preload stack rather than to the nut housing or servo tuning.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Restore rear bearing preload, lock the nut, and verify that the support block sits squarely against the saddle casting with the lube line and way-cover segment returned to their original paths. Confirm that the cover leaf no longer touches the screw and that the rear guide trucks remain lubricated rather than dry at the same travel zone.

step_records.surface_form: Restore rear bearing preload, lock the nut, and verify that the support block sits squarely against the saddle casting with the lube line and way-cover segment returned to their original paths. Confirm that the cover leaf no longer touches the screw and that the rear guide trucks remain lubricated rather than dry at the same travel zone.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Repeat the warm-up cycle through the same feed range and reversal points, then compare hot vibration, screw temperature, and backlash with the pre-repair values. The corrected condition must hold through the actual motion window that created the complaint, not just during a short cold jog near home.

step_records.surface_form: Repeat the warm-up cycle through the same feed range and reversal points, then compare hot vibration, screw temperature, and backlash with the pre-repair values. The corrected condition must hold through the actual motion window that created the complaint, not just during a short cold jog near home.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Inspect the rear bearing block, way-cover leaf, lube manifold fittings, and guide-truck area again after the repeated motion cycle because settling after travel often reveals a loose fastener or displaced cover segment. This second inspection confirms that the restored preload did not simply shift the symptom to an adjacent support part.

step_records.step_summary: Inspect the rear bearing block, way-cover leaf, lube manifold fittings,
step_records.surface_form: Inspect the rear bearing block, way-cover leaf, lube manifold fittings, and guide-truck area again after the repeated motion cycle because settling after travel often reveals a loose fastener or displaced cover segment. This second inspection confirms that the restored preload did not simply shift the symptom to an adjacent support part.

task.label: T9
task.surface_form: Close the work only after the record names the exact boundary, such as rear fixed-bearing preload at BR-YR1, locknut movement, cover interference, guide-truck lubrication loss, or no defect found at the checked support, and ties it to measured vibration and axial float. Accept the repair when Y-axis reversal remains smooth through the original warm motion window and the rear support hardware holds its corrected position.
